New Eden takes its name from a single star system. The system called New Eden contains the ruined EVE Gate, the dead wormhole that once led back to Earth.
Every human in New Eden descends from colonists who crossed the EVE Gate from the Milky Way, long before recorded cluster history begins.
When the EVE Gate collapsed, it stranded the colonies and broke their technology. Civilization needed millennia to claw its way back into space.
Violent electromagnetic storms still rage around the ruined EVE Gate. Ships that fly too close are torn apart.
Capsuleers fly their ships from a fluid-filled pod wired directly into the brain. Regular crews call them eggers.
When a pod is breached, a fatal brain scan captures the pilot's mind and transmits it to a fresh clone. The pilot dies and wakes up in the same moment.
Capsule technology was invented by the reclusive Jove, who handed it to the Caldari. That single deal made capsuleer immortality possible.
Amarr is the largest of the four empires, ruling roughly 40 percent of inhabited space in the name of a divinely appointed emperor.
For centuries the Amarr enslaved the entire Minmatar people. Many Minmatar remain in Amarr chains today, and the Republic has never forgiven it.
The Minmatar Great Rebellion broke Amarr rule and founded the Republic, striking while the Amarr fleet reeled from its defeat by the Jove.
Amarr once attacked the Jove at Vak'Atioth and was crushed by a technologically superior fleet. It remains the Empire's most humiliating defeat.
Heirs who lose the Amarr succession are expected to commit ritual suicide. King Khanid the Second refused, and the breakaway Khanid Kingdom was born.
Empress Jamyl Sarum performed the Amarr succession suicide, then reappeared alive five years later. A hidden clone had cheated the ritual.
In the year YC 110, a Minmatar Elder fleet crippled CONCORD and stormed into Amarr space. Jamyl Sarum vaporized it with one shot from an ancient superweapon.
Drifters ambushed and killed Empress Jamyl Sarum aboard her own titan. The Amarr throne then passed to Catiz the First.
The Caldari State has no conventional government. Eight megacorporations run everything, and citizenship is a job contract.
Caldari Prime does not sit in Caldari space. The Caldari homeworld orbits in Luminaire, the very same system as the Gallente homeworld.
The Caldari helped found the Gallente Federation, then seceded from it. The split ignited a war that still shapes both nations.
Caldari admiral Tovil-Toba crashed his burning carrier into Gallente Prime, buying time to evacuate Caldari Prime. He is revered as a martyr.
During the Battle for Caldari Prime, a Leviathan-class titan was shot down in orbit and its wreckage fell onto the planet below.
Tibus Heth, a factory worker turned demagogue, seized control of the entire Caldari State and retook Caldari Prime from the Gallente.
Gallente Federation is the only genuine democracy among the four empires, and millions of ethnic Caldari still live peacefully inside it.
Minmatar are the most numerous people in New Eden, yet huge numbers live outside the Republic, scattered, exiled, or enslaved.
Seven tribes form the Minmatar people. One tribe, the Starkmanir, was thought exterminated by the Amarr until survivors were found generations later.
Thukker tribe Minmatar live as permanent nomads, roaming the Great Wildlands in vast caravans with no fixed home at all.
Ammatar are Minmatar who sided with the Amarr during the rebellion. To most other Minmatar, the name is a synonym for traitor.
CONCORD firepower rests on technology gifted by the Jove, which is why fighting back against a response fleet is considered suicide.
Jove were the most advanced humans in New Eden, yet they hid from everyone. Their regions appear on star maps, but no gate will take you there.
A genetic affliction called the Jovian Disease dooms victims to fatal, unshakable apathy. It drove the brilliant Jove to the edge of extinction.
Sleepers of wormhole space descend from an ancient Jovian offshoot. Their minds retreated into virtual worlds while drones still guard the hardware.
Wormhole space has an official name, Anoikis. It holds roughly 2,600 star systems that appear on no map and contain no stargates.
Thera is a wormhole system with fully staffed stations inside it, operated by the Sisters of EVE. It is among the largest star systems known.
Sisters of EVE are a humanitarian order obsessed with studying the EVE Gate. They also build some of the finest exploration ships flying.
In the Seyllin incident, a stellar event obliterated an inhabited planet. That same day, wormholes tore open across the entire cluster.
Sansha Kuvakei converts captives into True Slaves, implanted drones stripped of free will. His Nation raids the empires through artificial wormholes.
Sansha Kuvakei was once hailed as a visionary reformer. The empires jointly destroyed his utopia after discovering it ran on mind-wiped slaves.
Blood Raiders are a cult that harvests human blood, and they prize the blood inside capsuleer clone vats above every other vintage.
Guristas, among the deadliest pirate factions alive, was founded by just two Caldari Navy deserters, call signs Fatal and the Rabbit.
Angel Cartel rules the lawless Curse region, and its sleek ship designs are widely suspected of borrowing hidden Jovian technology.
Serpentis began as a legitimate research company before becoming New Eden's largest producer of illegal combat boosters.
Mordu's Legion is an elite mercenary army built from Intaki veterans. Even empire navies treat its founder Muryia Mordu with respect.
Rogue drones are self-replicating machines that escaped their creators. They build hives in deep space and treat passing ships as raw material.
Triglavians of Abyssal Deadspace organize everything in threes. Their broadcasts come from three great clades speaking as one collective.
In 2020 the Triglavian Collective invaded known space and permanently seized 27 star systems, rewiring their stars to form the region of Pochven.
Titans are the largest ships a capsuleer can fly, with hulls measured in kilometers. A Caldari Leviathan stretches well over ten kilometers.
Each empire fields exactly one titan design: the Amarr Avatar, the Caldari Leviathan, the Gallente Erebus, and the Minmatar Ragnarok.
A titan carries a doomsday device, a superweapon that can delete another capital ship in a single firing sequence.
The first titan ever destroyed was an Avatar nicknamed Steve, lost in 2006. Its wreck is preserved in space as a permanent landmark.
The Bloodbath of B-R5RB in 2014 destroyed 75 titans in 21 hours, worth around 300,000 real dollars. It started over one unpaid sovereignty bill.
Wrecks from the Bloodbath of B-R5RB were never cleaned up. The battlefield remains in the game as a permanent monument named Titanomachy.
The Battle of Asakai ignited because one titan pilot misclicked, jumping his ship alone into a hostile fleet instead of opening a bridge.
More than 4,000 players fought in one star system at 6VDT-H in 2013, the largest battle the game had ever seen at the time.
Fury at FWST-8 in 2020 entered Guinness World Records as the largest player versus player battle in videogame history, with 8,825 participants.
Twin battles at M2-XFE during World War Bee 2 destroyed hundreds of titans and surpassed B-R5RB as the costliest fight in gaming history.
World War Bee in 2016 saw a mercenary coalition, bankrolled largely by in-game casino money, drive Goonswarm out of its northern empire.
Guiding Hand Social Club spent a year infiltrating a corporation, then assassinated its CEO and stole assets worth thousands of real dollars.
Band of Brothers, once EVE's most feared alliance, dissolved overnight when a single director defected to Goonswarm and disbanded it from within.
Chribba became EVE's most trusted player by brokering deals worth trillions. He also famously mined ore in high security space with a dreadnought.
Molea system hosts a cemetery in space. Players leave frozen corpses and containers there as memorials, some honoring pilots who died in real life.
Sean Smith, an American diplomat killed in Benghazi in 2012, was Goonswarm's famed diplomat Vile Rat. Players renamed stations across space in his honor.
A real bronze monument to EVE players stands in Reykjavik harbor, engraved with the names of thousands of pilots active in 2014.
Players elect a real Council of Stellar Management, and the winners are flown to Iceland to advise the developers on the game's future.
During the 2011 riots, players shot the Jita monument for days. Developers later scarred the monument permanently to commemorate the protest.
Jita is New Eden's great marketplace. A single station at its fourth moon anchors the cluster's trade, and the system often holds over a thousand pilots.
Burn Jita was an annual Goonswarm event that blockaded the busiest trade hub in the game, suicide ganking freighters under CONCORD's nose.
Hulkageddon was a recurring player event devoted to destroying as many mining ships as possible, with prizes for the top gankers.
Everyone plays in one universe. EVE runs on a single shard called Tranquility, so every war and market crash is shared by the whole world.
China runs a separate copy of New Eden, a second shard named Serenity, with its own alliances, its own history, and its own wars.
When battles grow enormous, time itself slows down. Time dilation can stretch the action to one tenth speed so the server can keep up.
The in-game currency ISK shares its abbreviation with the Icelandic krona, the real money of the country where EVE was built.
Real economists have worked on the developer payroll to watch over New Eden, publishing monthly reports on inflation, trade, and money supply.
Before EVE existed, its Icelandic creators funded the studio partly by selling a board game door to door across Iceland.
New Eden has run continuously since May 2003, one persistent universe carrying more than two decades of unbroken player history.
Skills train in real time whether a pilot is logged in or not. The longest skills take months of simply waiting.
Buying and selling entire characters is perfectly legal in EVE, through an official bazaar, as long as the price is paid in ISK.
Nearly every ship, module, and round of ammunition destroyed in EVE was manufactured by players, starting from ore that players mined.
Frozen corpses of podded pilots drift where they died. They can be scooped, traded, and collected, and some players hoard thousands.
Project Discovery turns downtime into science. EVE players have classified real proteins, hunted exoplanets, and analyzed COVID-19 blood samples.
Through PLEX for Good drives, players convert in-game wealth into real charity, raising hundreds of thousands of dollars for disaster relief.
PLEX can be traded for game time, meaning a sharp enough trader never pays a real subscription. Whole careers are funded from the market alone.
Dust 514 linked a console shooter to EVE. Capsuleers in orbit fired real orbital strikes to support mercenaries fighting on the planet below.
EVE Echoes carries New Eden onto phones, a parallel version of the universe with its own player empires and its own wars.
Aura is the artificial intelligence installed in every capsuleer vessel, the calm shipboard voice that greets each newly licensed pilot.
Quafe is New Eden's inescapable soft drink. The brand is so universal that even pirate crews and drifting outlaws stock it.
The Scope is a Gallente news network, and real player-driven wars have been reported in its broadcasts as galactic current events.
New Eden's calendar counts years from the Yoiul Conference. Year YC 105 lines up with 2003, the year capsuleers first appeared.
Known space contains around 5,000 star systems linked by stargates, with thousands more hidden away in unmapped wormhole space.
High security space is not safe, only avenged. CONCORD guarantees retribution for an attack, never the victim's survival.
In null security space no empire law exists at all. Player alliances claim the stars, building their own stations and jump gate networks.
Keepstar citadels are the largest structures players can build, fortresses so vast that entire titans dock inside them.
No one has ever built the Palatine Keepstar. Only one may exist in the whole universe, and its price runs to trillions of ISK.
Warp drives carry ships across a star system many times faster than light, while stargates handle the leaps between the stars themselves.
Deadspace pockets are regions where normal warp travel fails. Ships crawl in through acceleration gates, which is why pirates love hiding there.
Sansha incursions began in 2011, with Nation fleets seizing entire constellations until capsuleers banded together to drive them out.
Before Catiz the First took the Amarr throne, capsuleer champions dueled in televised trials held in the name of the royal heirs.
Amarr call their divine mission the Reclaiming, a mandate to gather all of humanity under God, by conversion or by chains.
Amarr emperors rule for life, and life can be long. Emperor Heideran the Seventh held the throne for more than a century.
Deleted pilots are not simply erased. Every biomassed character is transferred to a corporation named Doomheim, EVE's graveyard of the fallen.
Once a day the entire universe blinks out. Daily downtime pauses New Eden for a few minutes, and fleets plan their operations around it.
Alliance Tournament teams battle on a live broadcast stage for prize ships so rare that a single hull can be worth hundreds of billions of ISK.
Society of Conscious Thought, a secretive order with Jovian roots, has gifted Gnosis battlecruisers to pilots on EVE's anniversaries.
Building a titan is an industrial campaign. Construction consumes weeks of time, mountains of minerals, and dedicated nullsec infrastructure.
Stargates work in bound pairs separated by light years, and building a new pair is so monumental that the star map stays stable for decades.
The Rifter, a scrappy Minmatar frigate that looks welded together from spare parts, became one of the most beloved ships in the game.
Minmatar engineering embraces salvage and improvisation. Their warships fly with visible rust and mismatched plating, and pilots adore them for it.
CONCORD was founded jointly by the five great empires, including the reclusive Jove, to keep the peace between nations that barely trusted each other.
A capsule replaces most of a warship's bridge crew. One immortal mind can fly a battleship that would otherwise need hundreds of hands.
In EVE's first year, pirate players actually fought and destroyed CONCORD police ships. The developers responded by making CONCORD invincible forever.
